tasks.prompt: [Warm-up] Enable the Ingress Controller add-on in minikube and confirm it is actually running. Wait until the ingress-nginx Pod reaches Running status, then identify which namespace the controller Pod is deployed in.
tasks.starter:
# minikube must already be running
# Assumes the cluster is ready (minikube start has been run)

# Starting from a state with no Ingress-related resources
kubectl get pods -A | grep ingress
# (no output is expected at this point)
tasks.solution:
# 1. Enable the Ingress add-on
minikube addons enable ingress

# 2. Wait until the controller Pod in the ingress-nginx namespace is ready
kubectl wait --namespace ingress-nginx \
  --for=condition=ready pod \
  --selector=app.kubernetes.io/component=controller \
  --timeout=120s

# 3. Verify the controller Pod and Service
kubectl get pods -n ingress-nginx
kubectl get svc -n ingress-nginx

# Expected output (Pod)
# NAME                                        READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
# ingress-nginx-controller-xxxx               1/1     Running   0          60s

# Expected output (Service)
# NAME                                 TYPE        CLUSTER-IP      ...
# ingress-nginx-controller             NodePort    10.96.xxx.xxx   ...
tasks.explanation: minikube does not ship with an Ingress Controller pre-installed. The `minikube addons enable ingress` command deploys the ingress-nginx controller as a Deployment in the ingress-nginx namespace. Without the controller, no amount of Ingress resources will route any traffic, so this step must always come first. Using `kubectl wait` to check the Pod Ready condition — rather than just listing pods — builds a good habit of confirming the controller is fully up before moving on, saving you the 'why isn't it working?' debugging that follows if you skip ahead.
hints: Run `minikube addons list` to see currently enabled add-ons. Check that the STATUS column for the ingress row shows 'enabled'.

If `kubectl wait` times out, inspect the Pod with `kubectl describe pod -n ingress-nginx <pod-name>` and look at the Events section. An ImagePullBackOff error usually points to a network or firewall issue.
If the controller Service shows TYPE NodePort, that is correct. In a minikube environment, NodePort is used instead of LoadBalancer.

tasks.prompt: [Core Lab 1] Deploy two Deployments and their ClusterIP Services. The first, 'marvel-app', acts as the main page; the second, 'pay-app', acts as the payment page. Both Deployments use the nginx image, but each must return a distinct response — inject a custom index.html into each container via a ConfigMap. After deploying, confirm everything is up with `kubectl get pods` and `kubectl get svc`.
tasks.starter:
# Complete the YAML skeletons below.
# The key is using a ConfigMap to overwrite /usr/share/nginx/html/index.html
# in each nginx container so they return different content.

# marvel-configmap.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: marvel-html
data:
  index.html: |
    # TODO: add a single HTML line that identifies this as the Marvel main page

---
# pay-configmap.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: pay-html
data:
  index.html: |
    # TODO: add a single HTML line that identifies this as the Pay payment page
tasks.solution:
# ---- marvel-app.yaml ---- apiVersion: v1 kind: ConfigMap metadata: name: marvel-html data: index.html: |

Marvel Main Page

--- apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment metadata: name: marvel-app spec: replicas: 1 selector: matchLabels: app: marvel template: metadata: labels: app: marvel spec: containers: - name: nginx image: nginx:stable-alpine ports: - containerPort: 80 volumeMounts: - name: html mountPath: /usr/share/nginx/html volumes: - name: html configMap: name: marvel-html --- apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata: name: marvel-service spec: selector: app: marvel ports: - port: 80 targetPort: 80 # ---- pay-app.yaml ---- apiVersion: v1 kind: ConfigMap metadata: name: pay-html data: index.html: |

Pay Service Page

--- apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment metadata: name: pay-app spec: replicas: 1 selector: matchLabels: app: pay template: metadata: labels: app: pay spec: containers: - name: nginx image: nginx:stable-alpine ports: - containerPort: 80 volumeMounts: - name: html mountPath: /usr/share/nginx/html volumes: - name: html configMap: name: pay-html --- apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata: name: pay-service spec: selector: app: pay ports: - port: 80 targetPort: 80 # Apply kubectl apply -f marvel-app.yaml kubectl apply -f pay-app.yaml # Verify kubectl get pods # NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE # marvel-app-xxxx 1/1 Running 0 30s # pay-app-xxxx 1/1 Running 0 28s kubectl get svc # NAME TYPE CLUSTER-IP PORT(S) AGE # marvel-service ClusterIP 10.96.xxx.xxx 80/TCP 30s # pay-service ClusterIP 10.96.xxx.yyy 80/TCP 28s
tasks.explanation: Ingress references cluster-internal Services as its backends. ClusterIP is sufficient — the Ingress Controller runs inside the same cluster, so it can reach ClusterIP addresses directly. There is no need to expose these Services externally, making ClusterIP the correct choice for Services sitting behind an Ingress. The ConfigMap-based index.html injection is necessary so that the two services return visibly different responses, letting you confirm that routing is actually working.
hints: Set `volumeMounts.mountPath` to `/usr/share/nginx/html` so nginx uses that directory as its web root. A single character typo in the path will cause the default nginx page to be served instead.
Run `kubectl describe pod <pod-name>` and check the Events section to see whether the ConfigMap mount succeeded. A 'MountVolume.SetUp failed' message almost always means a typo in the ConfigMap name.
Verify that the Service is selecting the right Pods with `kubectl get endpoints marvel-service`. The ENDPOINTS column should show a Pod IP:80 entry.

tasks.prompt: [Core Lab 2] Write and apply a path-based Ingress resource using the `networking.k8s.io/v1` API. The `/` path must route to marvel-service and `/pay` must route to pay-service. Set `pathType` to `Prefix` and `ingressClassName` to `nginx`. After running `kubectl apply`, wait for `kubectl get ingress` to show an ADDRESS, then use curl (or the minikube service URL) to call both paths and confirm they return different responses.
tasks.starter:
# Complete the skeleton below to produce marvel-ingress.yaml
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: marvel-ingress
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target: /
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx
  rules:
    - http:
        paths:
          - path: /
            pathType: # TODO
            backend:
              service:
                name: # TODO
                port:
                  number: # TODO
          - path: /pay
            pathType: # TODO
            backend:
              service:
                name: # TODO
                port:
                  number: # TODO
tasks.solution:
# ---- marvel-ingress.yaml (completed) ---- apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1 kind: Ingress metadata: name: marvel-ingress annotations: nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target: / spec: ingressClassName: nginx rules: - http: paths: - path: / pathType: Prefix backend: service: name: marvel-service port: number: 80 - path: /pay pathType: Prefix backend: service: name: pay-service port: number: 80 # Apply kubectl apply -f marvel-ingress.yaml # Wait for ADDRESS to be populated (10-30 seconds) kubectl get ingress # NAME CLASS HOSTS ADDRESS PORTS AGE # marvel-ingress nginx * 192.168.49.2 80 15s # Verify routing (replace INGRESS_IP with the ADDRESS value above) export INGRESS_IP=$(kubectl get ingress marvel-ingress -o jsonpath='{.status.loadBalancer.ingress[0].ip}') curl http://$INGRESS_IP/ # Expected output:

Marvel Main Page

curl http://$INGRESS_IP/pay # Expected output:

Pay Service Page

# Alternative if IP is not populated in minikube minikube ip # Use the returned IP directly: curl http:///
tasks.explanation: Three fields are the heart of the v1 Ingress syntax. First, `apiVersion` must be `networking.k8s.io/v1` — `extensions/v1beta1` was removed in Kubernetes 1.22. Second, `ingressClassName` identifies which controller should handle this Ingress; ingress-nginx registers itself under the class name 'nginx'. Third, `pathType` is a required field in v1. `Prefix` means `/pay` matches `/pay`, `/pay/card`, `/pay/bank`, and any other subpath. The `rewrite-target: /` annotation rewrites the incoming `/pay` path to `/` before forwarding to pay-service, so the nginx backend serves its index.html rather than trying to resolve a `/pay` file path.
hints: If the ADDRESS column in `kubectl get ingress` remains empty, the controller may still be processing the Ingress or may not be in Running state. Re-check `kubectl get pods -n ingress-nginx`.
A 504 Gateway Timeout from curl means the Ingress received the request but could not reach the backend Service. Run `kubectl get endpoints marvel-service` to confirm that a Pod IP is registered.

To see the difference between `pathType: Prefix` and `pathType: Exact` hands-on, change the `/pay` rule to `Exact` and call `curl http://$INGRESS_IP/pay/test`. With `Exact` you get a 404; with `Prefix` you get a response.

tasks.prompt: [Advanced] Convert the path-based Ingress to host-based routing. Modify the Ingress so that `shop.example.com` routes to marvel-service and `pay.example.com` routes to pay-service. To test locally without real DNS, either add entries for the minikube IP to your hosts file (`/etc/hosts` on Linux/Mac, `C:\Windows\System32\drivers\etc\hosts` on Windows) or use curl's `--resolve` option. Send a curl request to each domain and confirm you receive different service responses.
tasks.starter:
# Modify the existing marvel-ingress.yaml for host-based routing.
# The difference from path-based: each entry in the rules array now has a host field,
# and each host has a single path: / rule.

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: marvel-ingress
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx
  rules:
    - host: shop.example.com
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /
            pathType: # TODO
            backend:
              service:
                name: # TODO
                port:
                  number: # TODO
    - host: pay.example.com
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /
            pathType: # TODO
            backend:
              service:
                name: # TODO
                port:
                  number: # TODO
tasks.solution:
# ---- marvel-ingress-host.yaml (completed) ---- apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1 kind: Ingress metadata: name: marvel-ingress spec: ingressClassName: nginx rules: - host: shop.example.com http: paths: - path: / pathType: Prefix backend: service: name: marvel-service port: number: 80 - host: pay.example.com http: paths: - path: / pathType: Prefix backend: service: name: pay-service port: number: 80 # Apply over the existing Ingress kubectl apply -f marvel-ingress-host.yaml # Get minikube IP export MINIKUBE_IP=$(minikube ip) # Option A: curl --resolve (test without modifying the hosts file) curl --resolve shop.example.com:80:$MINIKUBE_IP http://shop.example.com/ # Expected output:

Marvel Main Page

curl --resolve pay.example.com:80:$MINIKUBE_IP http://pay.example.com/ # Expected output:

Pay Service Page

# Option B: edit /etc/hosts (Linux/Mac) # echo "$MINIKUBE_IP shop.example.com pay.example.com" | sudo tee -a /etc/hosts # curl http://shop.example.com/ # curl http://pay.example.com/ # Option C: Windows hosts file (Administrator PowerShell) # Add-Content C:\Windows\System32\drivers\etc\hosts "$MINIKUBE_IP shop.example.com pay.example.com" # curl http://shop.example.com/ # curl http://pay.example.com/ # Confirm routing rules kubectl describe ingress marvel-ingress # Verify that both hosts appear in the Rules section, each mapped to the correct service
tasks.explanation: The key insight here is the structural difference between path-based and host-based routing. Path-based routing places multiple paths under a single rules entry; host-based routing gives each host its own rules entry. The controller matches incoming requests against the rules in order, reading the Host header of the HTTP request — so as long as both domains point to the same Ingress Controller IP, the routing works correctly. The `--resolve` option tells curl to force a specific domain to resolve to a given IP without touching DNS or the hosts file, making it an ideal tool for local testing. In production, you would register an A record pointing the domain to the Ingress Controller's external (LoadBalancer) IP in your cloud or internal DNS.
hints: Check the Rules section of `kubectl describe ingress marvel-ingress` and confirm that Host, Path, and Backends are all filled in correctly. Empty rules almost always mean a YAML indentation error.

If curl returns a 404 'default backend' response, the Host header did not match any rule. Calling the Ingress directly by IP (without `--resolve`) sends the IP itself as the Host header, which will never match a host-based rule.

The `rewrite-target` annotation is not needed in host-based routing. In the path-based lab it was required because forwarding a `/pay` request to the backend without rewriting would cause nginx to look for a file at the `/pay` path and return a 404. With host-based routing, every domain enters at `/`, so no rewrite is necessary.
